content.text: The hooks system in Claude Code is a comprehensive lifecycle interception mechanism that lets you attach shell commands, HTTP endpoints, LLM prompts, or subagents to specific moments during a session — from before a prompt is processed all the way to session teardown.
Hook events are organized into three cadences that determine when they fire:
Additionally, subagent and task lifecycle events (
) fire within the agentic loop, and a set of
, etc.) fire outside the main loop at any time.
Below is the full set of hook events available in Claude Code:
When a session begins or resumes
When a prompt is submitted, before Claude processes it
When a user-typed slash command expands into a prompt; can block it
Before a tool call executes; can block it
When a tool is denied by the auto-mode classifier; return
After a full batch of parallel tool calls resolves, before the next model call
When a task is being created via
When a task is being marked as completed
When the turn ends due to an API error; output and exit code are ignored
When an agent team teammate is about to go idle
When Claude Code sends a notification
While assistant message text is streamed/displayed
When a configuration file changes during a session
When the working directory changes (e.g., after a
When a watched file changes on disk; the
When a worktree is being created; replaces default git behavior
When a worktree is being removed
When an MCP server requests user input during a tool call
After a user responds to an MCP elicitation, before the response is sent back
When an event fires, Claude Code evaluates a three-step chain: the
narrows which hook groups are candidates, the
field filters by tool name, agent type, or other metadata, and the
on individual handlers provides fine-grained matching using permission rule syntax:
event fires on every tool call,
limits the group to Bash tool calls only, and
prevents the script from even spawning unless the Bash subcommand matches
check is best-effort, so it is not a security boundary — use the permissions system for hard allow/deny enforcement.
Five types of handler can run when a matcher fires:
Run a shell script; receives JSON on stdin, returns decisions via stdout and exit codes
POST the event JSON to a URL; useful for integrating with external services and webhooks
Call a tool on an already-connected MCP server; text output treated as stdout
Send a prompt to a Claude model for a yes/no single-turn decision
Spawn a subagent that can use Read/Grep/Glob to verify conditions before returning a decision (experimental)
(or as the HTTP POST body) containing the event context. The structure differs by event, but tool events always include:
Success; no decision — normal permission flow applies
Soft error; Claude Code logs the failure but continues
, the tool call is denied; for async hooks with
, Claude is woken and shown the stderr
To return a decision or message, a hook handler writes a JSON object to stdout:
, a hook can inject additional context into Claude's prompt using
How a matcher string is evaluated depends on the characters it contains:
Fires on every occurrence of the event
Each event type matches against a different field:
uses literal filenames to build a watch list — not regex
MCP tools appear as regular tools in all tool events and follow the pattern
. To match all tools from a server, append
is compared as an exact string and will match nothing:
Command hooks support two background modes:
— the hook runs in the background without blocking the session. Useful for logging, telemetry, and notifications where the result doesn't affect Claude's next action.

— the hook runs in the background but wakes Claude when it exits with code 2, feeding the hook's stderr back as a system reminder so Claude can react. Useful for long-running validators, CI watchers, and external monitors.
Where you define a hook controls who it applies to:
to block user, project, and plugin hooks, while still distributing vetted hooks through a plugin marketplace.
field on a hook handler uses permission rule syntax for sub-command filtering, which is especially useful for Bash hooks. Leading
filter fails open (runs the hook) when a Bash command can't be parsed, it should not be used as a security control — it is a performance optimization to avoid unnecessary process spawns.
) send the event context to a Claude model for a yes/no evaluation:
) are experimental and spawn a full subagent that can use tools like

to perform multi-step verification before returning a decision. This is powerful for complex context-aware validations like checking whether a file being overwritten is tracked in git, or whether a dependency being added is on an approved list.
Default timeouts vary by event type and handler type, reflecting the latency expectations of each event:
events fire in latency-critical paths, so Claude Code enforces lower ceilings to keep the UI responsive. Always set an explicit
if your hook can block for variable amounts of time.
Run background test suite after code changes (wake Claude on failure):
— tool events fire on every tool call and can block execution, while async events like
fire outside the main loop and can't block Claude

hooks, it wakes Claude with the hook's stderr

enable LLM-grade reasoning inside the hook system, not just shell scripting
is the right pattern for background CI watchers, test runners, and monitors that need to feed failures back to Claude without blocking the session
